FT_IC1.evidence: Cross-sectional data from the May 2020 COVID-19 survey (COVID-19 Rapid Response Research Release) from the Adolescent Brain Cognitive Development (ABCD) Study; N = 5412 adolescents aged 10-14 years

FT_IC5.evidence: Original empirical cross-sectional analysis; multiple linear regression analyses estimated associations between mental health and resiliency factors (mental health, perceived stress, social support, coping behaviors) and total screen use, adjusted for sex, race/ethnicity, household income, parent education, study site

FT_IC6.evidence: Published peer-reviewed letter in JAMA Pediatrics, January 2022; original data

FT_IC2.evidence: Step 1 (broad search): No phone/SM-specific estimate found in results; paper reports only total screen use composite and general modalities. Step 2 (composite content): Total screen time composite includes: multiplayer gaming, single-player gaming, texting, social media, video chatting, browsing internet, and watching/streaming movies/videos/TV. Composite bundles TV (non-phone), streaming (non-phone device), unspecified gaming modalities, and other non-phone items without separate per-device breakout. Per binding clarifications: (1) multi-device aggregated time fails unless per-device breakdown exists; (2) gaming alone without explicit mobile/phone modality fails; (3) TV bundled without breakout fails. Composite is predominantly non-phone-modality inclusive (TV, streaming, gaming) without isolation of phone/SM only.

notes: Paper 12 measures mental health/resiliency as outcomes with total screen time; however, the screen time composite fails IC2 because it bundles non-phone modalities (TV, streaming video) with phone/SM items without separate breakout of phone-only use.

FT_IC1.evidence: Analyzed data from the Adolescent Brain Cognitive Development Study (N = 5335, ages 10-14) from May 2020 COVID-19 survey

FT_IC5.evidence: Original empirical cross-sectional analysis; compared parent-reported, adolescent-reported, and parent-adolescent differences in recreational screen time across 5 screen categories using paired t-tests and linear regression

FT_IC6.evidence: Published peer-reviewed article in Academic Pediatrics, April 2022; original data

FT_IC2.evidence: Paper is purely epidemiological and descriptive. It measures parent vs. adolescent reports of recreational screen time (texting, video chatting, social media, multiplayer gaming) and analyzes sociodemographic predictors (sex, age, race, parent marital status, education, income) of reporting DISCREPANCIES. The only outcome is the accuracy/agreement of measurement between reporters. Paper reports no association between screen time modalities and any individual-trait outcome variable (no neural, behavioral, health, cognitive, well-being, personality, substance use, sleep, or academic outcomes). Sociodemographic factors are correlates of measurement agreement, not outcomes paired with SM/phone use per IC2 definition.

notes: Paper 75 is a methodological/measurement validation study; it has no qualifying outcome variable. The focus is parent-child agreement on screen time estimates, not associations with individual-trait outcomes.

FT_IC1.evidence: Cross-sectional data from the Adolescent Brain Cognitive Development (ABCD) Study Year 2 (N = 9429) ages 11-12 years

FT_IC5.evidence: Original empirical cross-sectional analysis; multiple logistic regression analyses estimated associations between sociodemographic factors and cyberbullying victimization/perpetration, and between screen time modalities and cyberbullying victimization/perpetration

FT_IC6.evidence: Published peer-reviewed article in Academic Pediatrics, 2022; original data

FT_IC2.evidence: Per binding clarification in §2.4: 'Cyberbullying alone does not qualify. Cyberbullying counts only when paired with a separately-measured phone/SM variable that has its own coefficient.' Paper measures screen time (including internet, social media, texting, video chat, multiplayer gaming, single-player gaming, YouTube videos, TV/movies) as IVs predicting cyberbullying victimization and perpetration as DVs. Cyberbullying is the outcome, not a qualifying phone/SM variable on the other side of a relationship with an individual-trait outcome. The paper does not report a separate qualifying phone/SM variable paired with an individual-trait outcome—it reports cyberbullying as the outcome of screen exposure, which by binding rule does not qualify.

notes: Paper 76 is a cyberbullying epidemiology study; cyberbullying alone as an outcome fails IC2 per the binding clarification. The paper does not pair a phone/SM variable as IV with a separately measured individual-trait-level outcome (other than cyberbullying itself).
